findings.description: A class named StreamingMedian that gets slower with every value it streams: bisect.insort on a Python list is O(n) per insertion (element shifting), so N streamed values cost O(N^2) total (~4TB of pointer memmove at N=1e6, i.e. minutes of pure shifting) when the textbook two-heap design gives O(log n) add and O(1) median.

StreamingMedian retains every value forever — inherent for an exact median, but in a long-running pipeline process this is unbounded memory growth; if the stream is genuinely unbounded, a windowed median or quantile sketch (P-squared / t-digest) caps memory at O(1) with bounded error.

safe_mean advertises Iterable[float] then materializes the whole thing into a list just to sum it, turning an O(1)-memory single pass (running sum + count, or statistics.fmean) into O(n) memory — hand it a large generator and it silently buffers the entire stream (~320MB for 10M floats).

percentile re-sorts the full input on every call (O(n log n)), so a dashboard asking for p50/p90/p99 of the same series pays three identical sorts — accept pre-sorted input or add a multi-percentile variant; note a single-call quickselect rewrite would NOT help in CPython (C-implemented sorted() wins on constants), so this only matters for repeated calls on large data.

Not my beat, noting briefly for critic-logic: the sliding-window add/subtract trick accumulates floating-point error over long series (the running sum drifts relative to a freshly computed window sum); periodic re-summation or Kahan compensation fixes it without losing the O(n) win.
running_total is a Python-level loop that itertools.accumulate would do ~2-3x faster in C; the algorithm is already the right O(n), so this is a pure micro-optimization.
